tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.workaround: Try to remove indirect accesses. If applicable, precompute elements out of the innermost loop.
hint.details:
- Irregular (variable stride) or indirect: 1 occurrence(s)
Non-unit stride (uncontiguous) accesses are not efficiently using data caches
hint.title: Slow data structures access
hint.txt: Detected data structures (typically arrays) that cannot be efficiently read/written

hint.workaround:
- Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * Please look into your compiler manual for march=native or equivalent
 - Use vector aligned instructions:
  1) align your arrays on 32 bytes boundaries
  2) inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: read your compiler manual.
<<image_vec_align>>
hint.title: Vector unaligned load/store instructions
hint.txt: Detected 2 suboptimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.

hint.title: Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt: 3 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 16 FP arithmetical operations:
 - 8: addition or subtraction (4 inside FMA instructions)
 - 8: multiply (4 inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 160 bytes (20 double precision FP elements).

hint.title: Arithmetic intensity
hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.10 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

expert.title: General properties
expert.txt:
nb instructions    : 25
nb uops            : 24
loop length        : 117
used x86 registers : 10
used mmx registers : 0
used xmm registers : 3
used ymm registers : 3
used zmm registers : 0
nb stack references: 0
ADD-SUB / MUL ratio: 1.00

expert.help: Front-end corresponds to instructions fetching and decoding into macro/micro operations
expert.title: Front-end
expert.txt:
ASSUMED MACRO FUSION
FIT IN UOP CACHE
micro-operation queue: 7.50 cycles
front end            : 7.50 cycles

expert.help: Back-end corresponds to macro/micro operations execution (includes allocating registers, fetching operands etc.)
expert.title: Back-end
expert.txt:
| P0   | P1   | P2   | P3   | P4   | P5   | P6   | P7
--------------------------------------------------------------
uops   | 2.00 | 2.00 | 9.00 | 9.00 | 0.00 | 6.00 | 2.00 | 0.00
cycles | 2.00 | 2.00 | 9.00 | 9.00 | 0.00 | 6.00 | 2.00 | 0.00

Execution ports to units layout:
 - P0 (256 bits): VPU, BRU, ALU, DIV/SQRT
 - P1 (256 bits): ALU, VPU
 - P2 (256 bits): store address, load
 - P3 (256 bits): store address, load
 - P4 (256 bits): store data
 - P5: ALU
 - P6: ALU, BRU
 - P7: store address

Cycles executing div or sqrt instructions: NA
Longest recurrence chain latency (RecMII): 4.00

expert.title: Cycles summary
expert.txt:
Front-end : 7.50
Dispatch  : 9.00
Data deps.: 4.00
Overall L1: 9.00

expert.help: Proportion of vectorizable instructions that are actually vectorized. Higher is better.
expert.title: Vectorization ratios
expert.txt:
all     : 38%
load    : 20%
store   : NA (no store vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
mul     : 100%
add-sub : 100%
fma     : 100%
div/sqrt: NA (no div/sqrt vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
other   : 100%

expert.help: Effective/average relative length/width used in vector/SIMD registers. Higher is better.
expert.title: Vector efficiency ratios
expert.txt:
all     : 46%
load    : 40%
store   : NA (no store vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
mul     : 100%
add-sub : 100%
fma     : 100%
div/sqrt: NA (no div/sqrt vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
other   : 50%

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 9.00 cycles. At this rate:
 - 27% of peak load performance is reached (17.78 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.help: List of bottlenecks detected by CQA. A bottleneck is an issue which reduces front-end throughput below the core capacity.
expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks

header: 11% of peak computational performance is used (1.78 out of 16.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA)
gain.details:
38% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 20% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 46% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 9.00 to 6.00 cycles (1.50x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Read less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop
gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt:
Performance is limited by reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 9.00 to 7.50 cycles (1.20x speedup).

potential.workaround:
Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt:
Detected 4 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
